SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.IRON_FERRITIN_CONTENT:
Iron is a vital mineral for your child's growth, brain development, and immune system. Iron deficiency is one of the most common nutritional problems in childhood and should be carefully monitored, especially between 6 months and 3 years of age. Iron deficiency can lead to anemia, which may manifest as pallor, loss of appetite, easy fatigue, and difficulty concentrating.

Ferritin is an indicator of the body's iron stores. Even if hemoglobin levels are normal, low ferritin means iron stores are being depleted, and this condition requires treatment. Your doctor will order these tests during age-appropriate screening periods or when symptoms are noticed.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.IRON_FERRITIN_INFO: Excessive cow's milk consumption (over 600 mL per day) can reduce iron absorption in children, leading to hidden iron deficiency. Iron-rich foods include red meat, egg yolk, dark green leafy vegetables, and legumes.

SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.MR_CT_DETAIL_CONTENT:
MRI uses a powerful magnetic field to obtain detailed images of the brain, spinal cord, joints, and soft tissues. It contains no radiation and is safely used in children. However, since the procedure takes 20–45 minutes, sedation may sometimes be necessary for young children due to difficulty remaining still.

CT scan provides faster results but involves low-level radiation. It may be preferred in emergencies, head trauma, or when MRI cannot be performed. Your doctor will select the most appropriate imaging method by evaluating the advantages and disadvantages of each for your child's condition.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.MR_CT_DETAIL_INFO: Our clinic works in collaboration with Sonomed Ataşehir branch for necessary imaging procedures. We guide you through appointment scheduling and private health insurance authorization processes.

SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.NEWBORN_CHD_CONTENT: Using pulse oximetry, your baby's blood oxygen level adequacy is evaluated. This simple and painless test enables early diagnosis of certain serious congenital heart diseases.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.NEWBORN_DESC: Screening tests performed in the first days after birth enable early detection of rare but treatable conditions.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.NEWBORN_HEARING_CONTENT: Your baby's hearing is evaluated in the first days or weeks after birth using otoacoustic emission (OAE) or auditory brainstem response (ABR) tests. Early detection of hearing loss is extremely important for language and speech development.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.NEWBORN_HEEL_CONTENT: Performed 48–72 hours after birth by taking a few drops of blood from your baby's heel. It screens for metabolic diseases including phenylketonuria (PKU), congenital hypothyroidism, biotinidase deficiency, cystic fibrosis, and congenital adrenal hyperplasia.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.NEWBORN_INFO: Screening tests are risk assessments, not diagnoses. An abnormal result does not necessarily mean your baby has a disease; confirmatory tests may be needed. Your doctor will inform and guide you at every step.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.PREP_DESC: With proper preparation and approach, the blood draw process can be made much easier for your child.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.PREP_EMLA_CONTENT: At our clinic, EMLA cream (topical anesthetic) can be used to minimize needle pain for your child. This cream is applied to the blood draw site approximately 45–60 minutes before the procedure to numb the skin. It provides great comfort, especially for children with needle phobia.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.PREP_FASTING_CONTENT: If your doctor has ordered fasting tests, your child needs to fast for the specified duration. Generally 8–12 hours of fasting is required; however, this may differ for babies and young children. Water is generally permitted and even encouraged to facilitate blood draw.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.PREP_PSYCH_CONTENT: Explain what will happen in age-appropriate language, honestly but calmly. Rather than unrealistic promises like "it won't hurt at all," saying "it will sting a little but will be very brief" is a more reliable approach. For children over 3, you can role-play the process with a toy doctor set.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.PREP_RESULTS_CONTENT: Most routine blood tests (CBC, CRP, biochemistry) are completed the same day. Culture tests take 24–48 hours, and some specialized tests (allergy panels, hormone tests) may take several business days. Results are sent to you via email.

SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.STOOL_CONTENT:
When your child has prolonged diarrhea, abdominal pain, weight loss, or abnormalities in stool (blood, mucus, color changes), your doctor may order stool tests. These include investigation of intestinal parasites, detection of diarrhea-causing agents such as rotavirus and adenovirus, and fecal occult blood testing.

The stool sample is collected in a clean, dry container and brought to our clinic. Sample collection containers are available at our clinic. Multiple samples on consecutive days may be required for some tests.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.STREP_CONTENT:
When your child presents with sore throat, difficulty swallowing, and fever, your doctor may order a throat culture or rapid strep test. These tests determine whether the throat infection is caused by Group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus (GABHS) bacteria.

Proper identification and treatment of strep infection is very important because untreated cases can lead to serious complications such as acute rheumatic fever and associated heart valve disease. The rapid strep test provides results within minutes and can be performed at our clinic. Throat culture is the gold standard for definitive results, which are usually available within 24–48 hours.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.STREP_TITLE: Throat Culture and Rapid Strep Test
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.THYROID_CONTENT:
The thyroid gland is an important organ that produces hormones necessary for your child's growth, brain development, and metabolism. Congenital hypothyroidism is screened through the heel prick test in the newborn period. Follow-up thyroid function tests are ordered for babies with borderline results.

In older children, short stature, difficulty gaining weight or excessive weight gain, constipation, cold sensitivity, and low energy may indicate thyroid dysfunction. TSH and free T4 measurements show how well the thyroid gland is functioning.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.URINE_CONTENT:
Urinalysis is a test that investigates whether there are signs of infection, sugar, protein, or other abnormal substances in your child's urine. Urine culture determines the exact microorganism causing a urinary tract infection and identifies which antibiotic will be effective.

Urinary tract infections are common, especially in babies and young children, but symptoms may differ from adults. Unexplained fever, restlessness, loss of appetite, abdominal pain, or changes in urine odor may prompt your doctor to order these tests. Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial for protecting the kidneys.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.URINE_INFO: For babies in diapers, a urine collection bag is used; our clinic will guide you through this process. For toilet-trained children, a midstream urine sample is collected. A clean and proper sample is very important for reliable results.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.URTI_CONTENT:
Upper respiratory tract infections are the most common illness group in childhood. These infections present with fever, runny nose, cough, sore throat, and fatigue, and are mostly viral in origin. However, knowing which virus is responsible directly affects treatment planning.

Influenza (Flu) Test

The rapid influenza antigen test provides results within 15–20 minutes from a nasal or throat swab. It can distinguish between Influenza A and B types. Early diagnosis enables timely initiation of antiviral treatment such as oseltamivir (Tamiflu).

COVID-19 Test

The rapid antigen test provides results within 15 minutes from a nasal swab. PCR (RT-PCR) test is the gold standard for COVID-19 diagnosis with very high sensitivity.

RSV Test

RSV is the most common cause of lower respiratory tract infections in children under 2. The rapid RSV antigen test provides results within 15–20 minutes.

Adenovirus Test

Adenoviruses can cause pharyngitis, conjunctivitis, gastroenteritis, and sometimes urinary tract infections. A rapid antigen test can be performed from a nasal or throat swab.

Multiplex Respiratory Panel

Multiplex PCR panels can screen 15–20 different respiratory pathogens simultaneously from a single nasal swab. This comprehensive test is preferred for atypical presentations or co-infection suspicion.

Rapid Tests vs PCR: What's the Difference?

Rapid antigen tests detect viral proteins and provide results in 15–20 minutes but have lower sensitivity. PCR tests detect viral genetic material with much higher sensitivity and are the gold standard. When a rapid test is negative but clinical suspicion persists, PCR confirmation is recommended.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.USG_DETAIL_CONTENT:
Ultrasonography uses sound waves to visualize internal organs. It is painless, safe, contains no radiation, and can be safely repeated in children and babies.

Ultrasound TypeWhy and When Is It Performed?
Abdominal UltrasoundOrdered for evaluating liver, spleen, kidneys, and other abdominal organs in cases of abdominal pain, vomiting, or bloating.
Urinary System UltrasoundPerformed for structural evaluation of kidneys and bladder in children with urinary tract infections.
Hip Ultrasound (DDH Screening)Performed for developmental dysplasia of the hip screening in newborns and infants. Early detection is key to successful treatment.
Cranial UltrasoundUsed to evaluate brain structure in babies while the fontanelle is open. Painless and safe.
Soft Tissue UltrasoundPerformed for lymph node enlargement, thyroid evaluation, skin swelling, and masses.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.USG_DETAIL_INFO: Ultrasonography contains no radiation whatsoever and is entirely based on sound waves. It poses no risk to your child. You can be with your child during the procedure to provide comfort.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.VITAMIN_D_CONTENT:
Vitamin D is an essential vitamin for the healthy development of your child's bones, strengthening of teeth, and proper functioning of the immune system. Vitamin D insufficiency is quite common in children in our country, and this risk is even higher for children living in large cities who are not adequately exposed to sunlight.

The Ministry of Health recommends daily vitamin D supplementation for all babies from birth. Your doctor will measure vitamin D levels through a blood test to assess whether your child has adequate levels and adjust the supplement dose accordingly. Vitamin D deficiency can lead to rickets (bone softening), muscle weakness, and frequent infections.
SERVICE_LAB_IMAGING.XRAY_DETAIL_CONTENT:
Digital X-ray uses low-dose radiation to image bones and the chest cavity. In children, it is most commonly ordered for chest X-ray (suspected pneumonia or bronchitis) and bone X-ray (fractures, growth evaluation). Digital technology provides significantly lower radiation doses compared to conventional methods.

Bone age assessment is a special X-ray taken from the left hand and wrist to determine whether your child's skeletal development is consistent with their chronological age. Your doctor may order this test in cases of short stature or early/delayed puberty.

SERVICE_VACCINES.DETAIL_BCG_CONTENT:
The BCG vaccine protects against tuberculosis, especially severe forms in children (tuberculous meningitis and miliary TB).

Administration: Single dose at 2 months, intradermal injection on left shoulder. A small bump and scar (BCG scar) is expected.

Side effects: Redness, bump and scar at injection site (expected). Rarely, axillary lymph node swelling.
SERVICE_VACCINES.DETAIL_HEPA_CONTENT:
Hepatitis A is a liver infection spread through contaminated water and food. Symptoms include fever, fatigue, nausea and jaundice.

Administration: 2 doses — 18 months (1st) and 24 months (2nd). Intramuscular injection. Inactivated vaccine.

Side effects: Injection site pain, mild fever. Serious reactions are very rare.
SERVICE_VACCINES.DETAIL_HEPB_CONTENT:
Hepatitis B is a serious liver infection transmitted through blood and body fluids. When acquired in the newborn period, it becomes chronic in 90% of cases, potentially leading to cirrhosis and liver cancer.

Administration: 4 doses total — 1 at birth + 3 within the hexavalent vaccine at 2, 4, and 6 months. WHO recommends the first dose within 24 hours of birth.

Side effects: Mild pain/redness at injection site, mild fever. Serious allergic reactions are extremely rare (1 in a million).
SERVICE_VACCINES.DETAIL_HEXA_CONTENT:
Since April 2025, Turkey uses the hexavalent vaccine, protecting against 6 diseases with a single injection: Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Polio, Hib, and Hepatitis B.

Administration: 3-dose primary series (2, 4, 6 months) + 1 booster (18 months) = 4 doses total. Thigh muscle injection.

Side effects: Injection site pain/redness, mild fever, irritability. Rarely high fever (39°C+) or febrile seizure.
SERVICE_VACCINES.DETAIL_MMR_CONTENT:
The MMR vaccine protects against three diseases with one injection: Measles (highly contagious, pneumonia/encephalitis risk), Rubella (can cause birth defects in pregnancy), and Mumps (meningitis, hearing loss risk).

Administration: 2 doses — 12 months (1st) and 48 months/4 years (booster). Subcutaneous injection. Live attenuated vaccine.

Side effects: Mild fever, mild rash (7-12 days later). Rarely: febrile seizure (1 in 3,000 doses).
SERVICE_VACCINES.DETAIL_MMR_INFO: Comprehensive scientific studies have conclusively shown no link between the MMR vaccine and autism. This claim has been repeatedly debunked by the scientific community.
SERVICE_VACCINES.DETAIL_PCV_CONTENT:
Pneumococcus (Streptococcus pneumoniae) can cause pneumonia, meningitis, bloodstream infections, and middle ear infections in young children.

Administration: 2-dose primary series (2 and 4 months) + 1 booster (12 months) = 3 doses. Intramuscular injection.

Side effects: Redness/swelling at injection site, mild fever, irritability. Rarely high fever.
SERVICE_VACCINES.DETAIL_VARICELLA_CONTENT:
Chickenpox is a highly contagious childhood disease with itchy, fluid-filled blisters and fever. While usually mild, it can lead to skin infections, pneumonia, and encephalitis.

Administration: 1 dose in Turkey (12 months). CDC and many international bodies recommend 2 doses (12-15 months and 4-6 years) for full protection.

Side effects: Injection site redness, mild fever. Rarely: mild chickenpox-like rash.
SERVICE_VACCINES.DETAILS_DESC: Detailed information about each vaccine, the diseases they prevent, and possible side effects.
SERVICE_VACCINES.EXTRA_DESC: Vaccines not in the national schedule but strongly recommended by pediatricians. Their absence from the schedule is due to budget constraints, not safety concerns.
SERVICE_VACCINES.EXTRA_FLU_DESC: Recommended every fall/winter for all children 6 months and older. Vaccine formula is updated each season as the virus changes.
SERVICE_VACCINES.EXTRA_HPV_DESC: Protection against cervical, throat and other cancers. Recommended for both boys and girls. Ideally given before sexual activity begins.
SERVICE_VACCINES.EXTRA_MENINGO_DESC: Protection against meningitis and sepsis. Full protection requires two separate vaccines: MenACWY and MenB.
SERVICE_VACCINES.EXTRA_ROTAVIRUS_DESC: Protection against rotavirus, the most common cause of severe diarrhea and vomiting in infants. Given orally as drops from 2 months of age.
SERVICE_VACCINES.FAQ_A1: No. Dozens of large-scale studies covering millions of children have conclusively shown no causal link between vaccines and autism. The 1998 paper making this false claim was retracted for data fraud, and the author's medical license was revoked.
SERVICE_VACCINES.FAQ_A2: No. A baby's immune system can respond to thousands of different antigens simultaneously from birth. The antigen load from vaccines is a tiny fraction of what they encounter daily.
SERVICE_VACCINES.FAQ_A3: Natural infection may sometimes provide strong immunity, but the cost is the disease itself — measles encephalitis, pertussis brain damage, polio paralysis. Vaccines create similar immune responses without these risks.
SERVICE_VACCINES.FAQ_A4: Many diseases are rare precisely because of high vaccination rates. When rates drop, diseases return — the 35,000+ measles cases in Europe in 2024 prove this.
SERVICE_VACCINES.FAQ_A5: Post-vaccine mild fever (38-38.5°C) is a normal sign of immune response. It passes in 1-2 days. Fever reducers can be given as recommended by your doctor. Seek medical attention for fever above 40°C or lasting more than 3 days.
SERVICE_VACCINES.FAQ_A6: Yes. Premature babies should be vaccinated at the same chronological age as full-term babies. Their vulnerability to infections makes timely vaccination even more important.
SERVICE_VACCINES.FAQ_A7: Don't worry — you don't need to restart the vaccine program; just continue from where you left off. Contact your pediatrician to create a catch-up plan. Note: Rotavirus vaccine has a strict age limit for the first dose (no later than 12-15 weeks).

SERVICE_VACCINES.HERO_SUBTITLE: We provide personalized vaccine tracking aligned with the national immunization schedule and international recommendations.
SERVICE_VACCINES.INTRO_DESC: Vaccines are one of the greatest achievements in medical history. According to WHO, they prevent 3.5-5 million deaths worldwide every year. At our clinic, we meticulously track your child's vaccination schedule according to current national and international guidelines.
SERVICE_VACCINES.INTRO_TITLE: What Are Vaccines and Why Are They Important?
SERVICE_VACCINES.SAFETY_DESC: Modern vaccines are among the most rigorously safety-tested medical products in history.
SERVICE_VACCINES.SAFETY_SIDE_EFFECTS_DESC: Mild pain/redness at injection site; mild fever (38-38.5°C); irritability — these symptoms resolve in 1-3 days and indicate immune response.
SERVICE_VACCINES.SAFETY_TESTING_DESC: Every vaccine undergoes laboratory testing, three-phase clinical trials, national/international licensing, and continuous post-market surveillance.
SERVICE_VACCINES.SAFETY_WHEN_CALL_DESC: Seek immediate medical attention for: fever above 40°C, crying lasting more than 3 hours, seizures, facial/lip swelling, difficulty breathing, or widespread rash.
SERVICE_VACCINES.SCHEDULE_DESC: With the 2025 update, Turkey has transitioned to the hexavalent vaccine. Single injection protects against 6 diseases.
SERVICE_VACCINES.SCHEDULE_NOTE: All vaccines in this schedule are provided free of charge at Family Health Centers by the Turkish Ministry of Health.
SERVICE_VACCINES.SCHEDULE_ROW_6_VACCINES: Hexavalent (Booster), Hepatitis A (1st dose), OPV (2nd dose)
